title: Thirty Kids, One Bell Schedule
tagline: Six periods, one kid who's clearly not okay, and a bell that doesn't care either way.

wake_up.text: Lesson plans for the day are mostly ready — mostly. There's still the matter of forty ungraded quizzes waiting from yesterday.

choices.text: Finish grading before school instead of eating breakfast
Eat breakfast and grade at lunch instead

morning_prep.text: Classroom set up, first period's worksheets copied, the whiteboard still smells like yesterday's marker.

choices.text: Head to the door to greet first period

first_period.text: First period drags in half-asleep. One student is already asking to use the bathroom before the bell even finishes ringing.

choices.text: Greet every student individually at the door
Get straight into the lesson to use every minute

random_moment.text: The lesson moves along, and the small, unpredictable texture of a classroom fills in around it, a little differently every day.

choices.text: A student asks a genuinely great question that derails things productively
An unscheduled fire drill eats fifteen minutes
The whole class shares a genuine laugh about something small

second_period.text: A parent email arrives mid-period: a complaint about last week's homework load, cc'ing the department head.

choices.text: Draft a thoughtful reply during your prep period
Let it sit until after school

prep_period.text: Your one prep period of the day — forty-five minutes that could go toward grading, lesson planning, or catching your breath.

choices.text: Use it to grade the backlog of quizzes
Actually sit down and rest for a few minutes

cafeteria_duty.text: Lunch duty — a hundred and fifty kids, one cafeteria, and a noise level that makes your ears ring by the end of every shift.

choices.text: Get through it

afternoon_crisis.text: Fifth period, mid-lesson: a student who's normally quiet suddenly puts their head down and won't respond, shoulders shaking. The rest of the class has noticed.

choices.text: Stop the lesson and quietly check in with them right now
Keep the lesson going and check in privately after class
Quietly send them to the counselor's office right away
Message the front office to call home immediately

crisis_outcome_immediate.text: A quiet word, one on one, while the class works independently for a few minutes. It turns out to be exactly what was needed — the student opens up, just a little, and it matters.

choices.text: Follow up after class and loop in the counselor

crisis_outcome_continue.text: The lesson continues, and the follow-up after class happens forty-five minutes later than it maybe should have. The student is still there, at least, waiting.

choices.text: Have the delayed conversation

crisis_outcome_counselor.text: The counselor meets the student in the hallway within minutes — trained for exactly this, with more time and resources than a classroom mid-lesson allows.

choices.text: Continue the lesson for everyone else

crisis_outcome_call.text: The front office calls home. It takes the rest of the period to hear back, and the student sits through all of it without anyone actually talking to them directly.

choices.text: Continue the day

sixth_period.text: Last period of the day, the one where everyone — you included — is running on fumes.

choices.text: Push through the planned material anyway
Switch to a lighter review activity instead

after_school.text: The bell rings. A student lingers at your desk, clearly wanting to talk about something but not quite starting.

choices.text: Stay and give them the time, whatever else is waiting
Gently redirect them to tomorrow — you have a staff meeting

grading_evening.text: Home, dinner, and the same stack of ungraded quizzes waiting on the kitchen table that's been waiting all week.

choices.text: Grade thoroughly with real feedback on each one
Grade quickly with minimal comments

end_shift.text: Tomorrow's lesson plans are already half-formed in your head before tonight's are even finished being graded.

choices.text: Close the laptop and go to bed

triumphant.title: The One That Sticks
triumphant.blurb: Today had the kind of moment that's the entire reason people become teachers — the one a student will remember for years.

steady_hand.title: A Good Day's Work
steady_hand.blurb: Nothing dramatic, just consistent, caring teaching all day long. That adds up to more than it feels like in the moment.

ordinary_day.title: Six Periods, Done
ordinary_day.blurb: A full day, taught, survived, mostly graded. Tomorrow's lesson plan is already half-written.

burned_out.title: Running on Empty
burned_out.blurb: You made it through the day, but there's nothing left for tonight's grading, let alone yourself. This pace isn't sustainable.

written_up.title: A Conversation With the Principal
written_up.blurb: Something about how today was handled is going to come up in a meeting. No one was harmed. Questions are still coming.
